fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the team workspace management piece for the platform. Basically users should be able to create teams, add/remove people, switch between teams, and handle all the role/permission stuff. Think of it like that membership layer we built for the org module a while back, but more fleshed out.

A few things I know we need: when someone tries to do something they shouldn't, we need consistent error responses (not just random 500s or whatever). There's also some HTTP redirect behavior for the team-switching and role-update flows that needs to be wired up properly — the product team is getting complaints that the current prototype just hangs or throws a generic error page.

Also important: when a user deletes their account, we can't leave orphaned teams floating around — their owned teams should go away but teams owned by others should stay intact. Oh and permissions work in two layers — the role itself plus whatever the token was issued with — both have to line up for access to go through.

One more thing: duplicate members, missing users, owners trying to leave their own team — all of these need to surface a specific, normalized message, not just a boolean or a generic failure. The format needs to match what the frontend expects. Ask me if you're unsure about the exact shape of those error payloads.

trigger_keywords: permission list format
how are permissions returned
what does the permission output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Permissions must be de-duplicated across all registered roles and returned as a single comma-separated string sorted lexicographically (A-Z). The output also includes a has_permissions flag (yes/no) and the total role_count. Example: has_permissions=yes
permissions=create,delete,read,update
role_count=2

trigger_keywords: blank team name error
validation error format
what does a validation failure look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
All validation failures must be returned in the normalized three-line format: error=validation_failed
field=<field_name>
message=<human readable message>
. For a blank team name the field is 'name' and the message is exactly 'The name field is required.'

trigger_keywords: missing user email error
user not found when adding member
what happens when you add someone who isn't registered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When the email provided for team member addition does not match any registered user, return a normalized validation error: error=validation_failed
field=email
message=We were unable to find a registered user with this email address.

trigger_keywords: duplicate member error
adding the same user twice
user already on team
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When a user being added already belongs to the team, return: error=validation_failed
field=email
message=This user already belongs to the team.

trigger_keywords: owner leaving their own team
owner remove themselves
can an owner leave a team they created
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
An owner cannot remove themselves from a team they created. This must return a normalized validation error: error=validation_failed
field=team
message=You may not leave a team that you created.

trigger_keywords: unauthorized member removal
non-owner trying to remove a member
authorization error format for removal
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When an actor who is not authorized (e.g., a regular member) attempts to remove another member, the response must be: error=authorization_denied
 — no additional fields.

trigger_keywords: deleting personal team
can you delete a personal team
personal team deletion validation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Deleting a personal team is not allowed and must return: error=validation_failed
field=team
message=You may not delete your personal team.

trigger_keywords: HTTP status for team switch
redirect after switching team
what status code does team switching return
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A successful team switch by an authorized actor returns HTTP 303 and redirects to http://localhost/home. The output must include http_status=303, redirect_url=http://localhost/home, current_team_id_matches=yes, and is_current_team=yes. An unauthorized switch (actor does not belong to the team) returns http_status=403 and redirect_url=null.

trigger_keywords: HTTP status for role update
redirect after updating member role
what happens when the owner changes a member's role via HTTP
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A successful role update by an authorized actor (owner) returns HTTP 303 and redirects to http://localhost (not /home). The response includes http_status=303, redirect_url=http://localhost, the new role, and permission check results. An unauthorized attempt returns http_status=403 and redirect_url=null.

trigger_keywords: token scoped permissions
two-layer permission check
role permission AND token permission
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Permission resolution requires BOTH the team role permission AND the token's active permission scope to include the requested permission. If the token was issued with a scope that does not include the permission, access is denied even if the role grants it. Both conditions must be true simultaneously.

trigger_keywords: account deletion with teams
deleting a user who owns teams
orphaned teams after user deletion
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a user is deleted, all teams they own must also be deleted, and all their team memberships (on teams owned by others) must be removed. Teams owned by other users must remain intact. The expected output tracks teams_before, memberships_before, user_deleted=yes, teams_after, and memberships_after.

trigger_keywords: personal team on creation
is a newly created team personal
personal_team flag for new teams
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A team created via the team creation flow is NOT a personal team. The output must include personal_team=no. Personal team status is a distinct flag that is false by default for user-created teams.

trigger_keywords: current team after creation
personal team ID matches
what is the owner's current team
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: After a team is created and the owner is set up, personal_team_id_matches=yes and current_team_id_matches=yes must both be observable, indicating that the owner's current team reference and personal team reference correctly point to the created team context. The owner belongs to and owns the team (owner_belongs_to_team=yes, owner_owns_team=yes).

trigger_keywords: member removal event
event dispatched on removal
signal when a member is removed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a member is successfully removed from a team, a removal event signal must be dispatched. The output must include removed_event_dispatched=yes alongside the member_count_before and member_count_after values.

trigger_keywords: non-owner team deletion authorization
outsider trying to delete a team
only the owner can delete
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Only the team owner is authorized to delete a team. If a non-owner actor attempts deletion validation, the response must be: error=authorization_denied
 — no additional fields, same shape as unauthorized member removal.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The two-layer permission resolution logic described in Feature 5 (token_limited_team_permission scenario) where both the team role's permissions AND the token's active permission scopes must include the requested permission for access to be granted — see feature5_team_membership_permissions.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: consistent error responses the frontend expects
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
The normalized key=value newline-delimited output format used for all validation and authorization errors: error=validation_failed
field=<field>
message=<text>
 for validation, and error=authorization_denied
 for authorization failures — defined across features 4, 6, 7, 8, 9

context_pointers.vague_reference: the membership layer we built for the org module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The team membership and ownership relationship model covering belongs_to_team, owns_team, owned_team_count, all_team_count, personal_team, and current_team references — see feature5_team_membership_permissions.json scenario team_relationships

context_pointers.vague_reference: HTTP redirect behavior for team-switching and role-update flows
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8 (feature8_current_team_http_switching.json) expects HTTP 303 + redirect to http://localhost/home on success; Feature 9 (feature9_team_member_role_http_updates.json) expects HTTP 303 + redirect to http://localhost (no /home path) on success; both return 403 + null redirect on authorization failure

context_pointers.vague_reference: orphaned teams floating around after account deletion
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 10 (feature10_user_deletion_with_teams.json): deleting a user must cascade-delete their owned teams and remove their memberships from teams owned by others, verified via teams_before/after and memberships_before/after counts

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have full context on right now — you'll want to dig into the existing codebase or check with the engineering lead. Try searching for how similar cases are handled in the current implementation and match that pattern.

feature10_user_deletion_with_teams.json.description: Deleting a user who owns teams and also belongs to another team removes the user, removes their owned teams, and clears their team membership associations without deleting teams owned by others.

feature1_role_permission_registry.json.description: Registering multiple team roles exposes whether any permissions exist and returns the de-duplicated permission set in sorted order.

feature2_team_creation.json.description: Creating a team for an owner either persists a non-personal team with the submitted name or returns a normalized validation error when the name is blank.

cases.expected_output:
team_created=yes
team_name=Product Team
owner_email=owner@example.com
personal_team=no
owned_team_count=1

error=validation_failed
field=name
message=The name field is required.

feature3_team_name_updates.json.description: Updating a team name as its owner either persists the new name or returns a normalized validation error when the new name is blank.

cases.expected_output:
error=validation_failed
field=name
message=The name field is required.

feature4_team_member_addition.json.description: Adding a user to a team by email attaches the member with the selected role, while missing users and duplicate members return normalized validation errors.

cases.expected_output:
error=validation_failed
field=email
message=We were unable to find a registered user with this email address.

error=validation_failed
field=email
message=This user already belongs to the team.

feature5_team_membership_permissions.jso.description: Team ownership, membership, personal-team selection, role permissions, and token scopes combine to determine visible team access results.

cases.expected_output:
owner_belongs_to_team=yes
owner_owns_team=yes
owned_team_count=1
all_team_count=1
personal_team_id_matches=yes
current_team_id_matches=yes
outsider_belongs_to_team=no
outsider_owns_team=no
member_belongs_to_team=yes
member_owns_team=no
member_can_foo=yes
member_can_bar=no

feature6_team_member_removal.json.description: Removing team members updates team membership and emits a removal signal; attempts by an owner to remove themselves or by an unauthorized member to remove another member return normalized errors.

cases.expected_output:
error=validation_failed
field=team
message=You may not leave a team that you created.

feature7_team_deletion.json.description: Team deletion removes the team record; deletion validation permits an owner to delete a non-personal team and rejects personal teams or non-owner actors.

cases.expected_output:
error=validation_failed
field=team
message=You may not delete your personal team.

feature8_current_team_http_switching.jso.description: An authenticated HTTP request can switch to a team the actor belongs to and redirects home; switching to a team the actor does not belong to returns an authorization status.

feature9_team_member_role_http_updates.j.description: An authorized HTTP request can change a member role and updates permissions; an unauthorized member updating their own role receives an authorization status.
